{{Quote|This choral piece, which has to do with the sword fight and comes at the end of the film, is a result of my thinking that something ritualistic and/or pagan and antique might be very effective.|John Williams|<ref name="Interview1" />}}
"'''Duel of the Fates'''" is a [[Wikipedia:Motif (music)|musical theme]] composed by [[John Williams]] between [[October]] [[1998]] and [[February]] [[1999]] for the 1999 film [[Star Wars: Episode I The Phantom Menace|''Star Wars'': Episode I ''The Phantom Menace'']] and its respective [[Musical score|score]]. It was written to represent the [[Battle of Naboo#Duel in Theed|duel in Theed]] between the [[Sith]] [[Maul|Darth Maul]] and the [[Jedi Master]] [[Qui-Gon Jinn]] with his [[Padawan]] [[Obi-Wan Kenobi]] in the "Duel of the Fates" scene at the end of the movie. The composition was recorded by the [[London Symphony Orchestra]] (LSO) and the [[London Voices]] [[Wikipedia:Choir|choir]] in February 1999 in [[Wikipedia:EMI|EMI's]] [[Wikipedia:Abbey Road Studios|Abbey Road Studios]] in [[Wikipedia:London|London]], [[Wikipedia:England|England]]. The piece was used in all three of the [[prequel trilogy]] movies and included on the [[Wikipedia:Soundtrack album|soundtracks]] of [[Star Wars Episode I: The Phantom Menace (soundtrack)|''The Phantom Menace'']] and [[Star Wars Episode II: Attack of the Clones (soundtrack)|''Attack of the Clones'']], likewise also briefly appearing in the [[Star Wars Anthology Series|Anthology]] film ''[[Solo: A Star Wars Story]]'' and its accompanying soundtrack's deluxe edition. The motif is used many times throughout [[Star Wars video games|video game]]s, trailers, and numerous other pieces of [[Star Wars Legends|''Star Wars'' Legends]] media, as well as in the [[The Duel of the Fates|music video trailer]] for ''The Phantom Menace'', which includes footage of the theme's recording sessions.

The theme is mainly [[Wikipedia:Polyphony|polyphonic]], is in the [[Wikipedia:E minor|keys of E]] and [[Wikipedia:G minor|G minor]], and has a [[Wikipedia:Minor scale|minor]] mode, a [[Wikipedia:Tempo|tempo]] of 152 bpm, and a [[Wikipedia:Meter (music)|duple meter]] with a [[Wikipedia:Time signature|time signature]] of 4/4. The composition, which lasts four minutes and fourteen seconds, contains lyrical [[Wikipedia:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]] chants translated from the [[Wikipedia:Celtic|Celtic]] epic [[Wikipedia:Cad Goddeu|''Cad Goddeu'']]. The composition was made available for purchase on [[May 4]], 1999, with the release of the soundtrack for ''The Phantom Menace'', and the sheet music was released in the music books ''[[Music From Star Wars Episode I: The Phantom Menace]]'', ''[[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones: Selections from the Original Motion Picture Soundtrack]]'', ''[[Star Wars: A Musical Journey: Episodes I-VI]]'', ''[[Selections from Star Wars]]'', ''[[Star Wars Episodes I, II & III Instrumental Solos]]'', and ''[[Star Wars for Beginning Piano Solo]]''.

==Conception and development==
[[File:Ep1_OST.png|thumb|left|''Star Wars Episode I: The Phantom Menace'' soundtrack]]
[[John Williams]] wrote the [[Musical score|score]] for the [[1999]] film [[Star Wars: Episode I The Phantom Menace|''Star Wars'': Episode I ''The Phantom Menace'']] and the respective [[Star Wars Episode I: The Phantom Menace (soundtrack)|soundtrack]],<ref name="SW.com TPM">{{SWArchive|url=movies/episode-i|text=''Star Wars'': Episode I ''The Phantom Menace''}}</ref> as he had for the previous three ''[[Star Wars]]'' films of the [[original trilogy]].<ref name="SW.com ANH">{{SWArchive|url=movies/episode-iv|text=''Star Wars'': Episode IV ''A New Hope''}}</ref><ref name="SW.com ESB">{{SWArchive|url=movies/episode-v|text=''Star Wars'': Episode V ''The Empire Strikes Back''}}</ref><ref name="SW.com ROTJ">{{SWArchive|url=movies/episode-vi|text=''Star Wars'': Episode VI ''Return of the Jedi''}}</ref> He began work on the project in mid-[[October]] of [[1998]].<ref name="ST.COM" /> "Duel of the Fates" was written as the main theatrical motif for the film and was utilized in various forms throughout the scenes depicting the climax of the film during the [[Battle of Naboo]] where the [[Trade Federation]] forces battle the [[Royal Naboo Security Forces]]. The piece is used mainly to represent the [[Lightsaber duel|duel]] between [[Maul|Darth Maul]], a [[dark Lord of the Sith]], and the [[Jedi Master]] [[Qui-Gon Jinn]] and his [[Padawan|apprentice]], [[Obi-Wan Kenobi]].<ref name="TPM" />

When composing the theme, Williams felt something ritualistic and [[Wikipedia:Paganism|pagan]] would be very effective in evoking the proper emotions, so he took a stanza of text from the [[Wikipedia:Celtic|Celtic]] epic [[Wikipedia:Cad Goddeu|''Cad Goddeu'']]. He had friends from [[Wikipedia:Harvard University|Harvard University]] translate the English version back to Celtic, then to [[Wikipedia:Greek language|Greek]], and finally [[Wikipedia:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]], which he chose for its "beautiful sounds." Williams then reduced the stanza to phrases consisting of a single word when translated to English and repeated their Sanskrit counterparts.<ref name="Interview1" /> Williams composed the music for the motif on a [[Wikipedia:Piano|piano]]. The piece, along with the entire score, was performed by the [[London Symphony Orchestra]] and [[London Voices]]<ref name="TPMS" /> in [[Wikipedia:EMI|EMI's]] [[Wikipedia:Abbey Road Studios|Abbey Road Studios]]<ref name="BG" /> in [[Wikipedia:London|London]], [[Wikipedia:England|England]] in [[February]] 1999, both recording live at the same time. The motif was written for ''The Phantom Menace''{{'s}} end credits and then cut to fit in the picture.<ref name="ST.COM" />

===Release===
The [[The Duel of the Fates|music video]] for "Duel of the Fates" was premiered at the opening ceremony of [[Celebration (1999)|Celebration]] in [[Wikipedia:Denver|Denver]], [[Wikipedia:Colorado|Colorado]] on [[April 30]], 1999.<ref name="GC7,2">{{CollectorCite|7|A Celebration For The Ages}}</ref> In [[May]] 1999, [[Sony|Sony Signatures]] released "Duel of the Fates" as a [[Wikipedia:Compact disc|CD]] [[Wikipedia:Single (music)|single]] available only to [[Wikipedia:Radio station|radio stations]], [[Wikipedia:Television station|television stations]], and [[Wikipedia:MTV|MTV]].<ref name=":1">{{WebCite|url=http://www.atrl.net/trlarchive/?s=debuts|text=The TRL Archive - Debuts|date=2008-10-30|archivedate=20070928011304|nolive=1|agency=ATRL}}</ref><ref name="GC7" /> The original [[Wikipedia:Soundtrack album|soundtrack]], which includes the song, was released by [[Sony|Sony Classical]] on [[May 4]], 1999,<ref name="Amazon" /> and the Ultimate Edition on [[November 14]], [[2000]].<ref name="Amazon2" /> Also in 1999, the soundtrack was available for purchase as a special limited edition double vinyl album exclusively at towerrecords.com.<ref name="GC8">[[Star Wars Galaxy Collector 8|''Star Wars Galaxy Collector'' 8]], pg. 13</ref> The original soundtrack was re-released on [[February 6]], [[2012]] to coincide with the 3-D release of the film in theaters.<ref name="Amazon3" /> The album was also released on [[Wikipedia:Gramophone record|vinyl]] by "IAmShark" in [[2013]].<ref name="IAS" /> On [[September 25]], [[2015]], it was announced that Sony Classical would be releasing the soundtrack of ''The Phantom Menace'' along with those of the other five films in three new sets: the ''[[Star Wars: The Ultimate Vinyl Collection]]'', the ''[[Star Wars: The Ultimate Soundtrack Edition]]'', and the ''[[Star Wars: The Ultimate Digital Collection]]'' on [[January 8]], [[2016]].<ref name="SonyUE" />

==Summary==
{{Music1|Duel of the Fates.flac|Duel of the Fates|[[London Symphony Orchestra]]}}
"Duel of the Fates" is mainly [[Wikipedia:Polyphony|polyphonic]] and has a [[Wikipedia:Minor scale|minor]] mode, a [[Wikipedia:Tempo|tempo]] of 152 bpm for most of the piece, and a [[Wikipedia:Meter (music)|duple meter]] with a [[Wikipedia:Time signature|time signature]] of 4/4. The composition is four minutes fourteen seconds long<ref name="TPMS" /> and is in the keys of [[Wikipedia:E minor|E]] and [[Wikipedia:G minor|G minor]].<ref name="TPMMB" /> The theme commences in an E minor as [[Wikipedia:Homophony|homophonic]], [[Wikipedia:Maestoso|maestoso]] in style, with the London Voices singing a [[Wikipedia:Chant|chant]] in [[Wikipedia:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]. The tempo, marked as "allegro," then speeds up to 152 bpm as the [[Wikipedia:String section|strings]] enter with the [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Viola violas] playing a repeating [[Wikipedia:Phrase (music)|phrase]] consisting of two [[Wikipedia:Eighth note|eighth notes]] followed by two [[Wikipedia:Sixteenth note|sixteenth notes]] and another eighth note. The low strings play sets of one, two, and five eighth [[Wikipedia:Musical note|notes]] with a [[Wikipedia:Bar (music)|measure]] of [[Wikipedia:Rest (music)|rest]] between each set's measure excluding the last, which has a [[Wikipedia:Pickup note|pickup note]], making the previous measure only seven eights rest. This continues for twenty measures with slight variations in the low string part.<ref name="TPMS" />

[[File:John_Williams_DotF.png|thumb|left|250px|The London Symphony Orchestra performing the piece]]
After the phrase has repeated for six measures, the theme's main [[Wikipedia:Melody|melody]] comes in, played by the [[Wikipedia:Clarinet|clarinets]]. This melody consists of two eighth notes followed by four quarter notes, two more eighth notes, and a final quarter note. It repeats four times, with the first two occurrences being identical and the last two varying. The end quarter note in the last repeat of the melody is tied to two whole notes and crescendos as the strings continue to play their repeating phrases. After another three measures, the [[Wikipedia:Harp|harp]] plays a rising phrase. In the next bar, the [[Wikipedia:French horn|French horns]] play the melody, and the [[Wikipedia:Trombone|trombones]] echo it in the background. Then the strings repeat their phrases for a few more bars, this time accompanied by the [[Wikipedia:Flute|flutes]], and then the London Voices return with their Sanskrit chant. Meanwhile, the trombones play the motif's main melody. This is followed by more repetition of the string phrases intermingled with [[Wikipedia:Accent (music)|accented]] notes and phrases from the [[Wikipedia:Tuba|tubas]] and [[Wikipedia:Trumpet|trumpets]]. Then the London Voices return with the chant, and the French horns and trumpets trade out on the primary melody. The trumpets join the strings as they continue to repeat their phrases, [[Wikipedia:Dynamics (music)#Words/phrases indicating changes of dynamics|crescendoing]] into a chorus chant of two eighth notes followed by a quarter rest, which repeats eight times as the trumpets play between every other phrase. As the pitch increases to a G minor, the chant switches back to its original form, and the trumpets continue to play accented notes between phrases. The brass and strings then join in playing the strings' repeating phrases in unison.<ref name="TPMS" />

The piece becomes quiet with only the strings continuing as the pitch returns to E minor. Woodwinds come in with the theme's primary melody followed by the French horns and trumpets. The strings then start playing phrases composed of triplets. The main melody is traded between the flutes and French horns, and the orchestra grows into an instrumental version of the piece's second chant (played in a G minor). The chorus then returns singing the first chant. Between each pair of notes in the chant are the trumpets playing the string phrases and the French horns playing the theme's melody, with the trumpets playing between the first and third pairs and the French horns between the second and fourth. The second chant is then repeated, followed by the first quarter of the first chant, as the brass and strings once again join in playing the string phrases. The chant is repeated with a [[Wikipedia:Timpani|timpani]] roll in the middle. Next the entire orchestra plays the string phrases while crescendoing, followed by an upbeat trumpet part and a [[Wikipedia:Bongo drum|bongo]] part. The orchestra returns with the string phrases, and then the piece quiets as the strings continue their phrases and the woodwinds once again perform the melody. The Voices return with the first chant as it crescendos into the second chant. As the Voices sing the first quarter of the first chant, the trumpets play the string phrases. The timpani then plays a solo, the trumpets return with six eighth notes, the bongos roll, and the piece ends with one hit of the string phrase.<ref name="TPMS" />

==Use==
===In the soundtracks===
The theme makes its only pure occurrence in a ''Star Wars'' soundtrack in the second track titled "Duel of the Fates" on the ''Star Wars Episode I: The Phantom Menace'' soundtrack, but it is also briefly featured in the fifteenth track titled "Qui-Gon's Noble End." These are the only uses that are made of the theme in the original soundtrack; however, it is used several times throughout most of the last fourteen tracks of the Ultimate Edition soundtrack.<ref name="TPMS" /> The dialogue version, which is featured in the last track of the Ultimate Edition soundtrack and contains the audio from the [[The Duel of the Fates|"Duel of the Fates" music video]], was added to the end of the re-release of the original soundtrack, which became available in 2012 to coincide with the 3-D release of the film in theaters.<ref name="Amazon3" />

In the [[Star Wars Episode II: Attack of the Clones (soundtrack)|''Star Wars Episode II: Attack of the Clones'' soundtrack]], the motif makes one appearance three minutes and thirty-five seconds into the tenth track, titled "Return to Tatooine."<ref name="AOTCS" /> The [[Star Wars Rebels: Season Two (Original Soundtrack)|soundtrack]] for [[Star Wars Rebels Season Two|''Star Wars Rebels'' Season Two]] includes references to the theme fifty-five seconds into the track "Blinded."<ref name="SWR S2 OST" />

===In the movies===
[[File:DotF_TPM.png|thumb|right|250px|The "Duel of the Fates" scene]]
"Duel of the Fates" is first played in ''Star Wars Episode I: The Phantom Menace''. The "Duel of the Fates" scene, so named in the menus for the DVD version of the film, is the scene where the [[Jedi]] characters Qui-Gon Jinn and Obi-Wan Kenobi duel the [[Sith Lord]] Darth Maul in the [[Theed power generator]] on the [[planet]] [[Naboo]]. It is played in instrumental form as another major character, [[Queen]] [[Padmé Amidala|Amidala]], is ambushed along with her guards by [[battle droid]]s with rolling capabilities and shields called [[droideka]]s in the [[Theed Hangar/Legends|Theed Hangar]], and as Darth Maul and the Jedi activate their [[lightsaber]]s at the commence of their duel. It is used again as the duel moves from the hangar to a generator complex. The cue comes to an end as [[plasma]] shields separate the three combatants for the first time; however, the theme appears shortly after as the [[youngling]] [[Anakin Skywalker]] destroys the [[Lucrehulk-class Battleship|droid control ship]] above the planet. The original recording is used during the film's end credits.<ref name="TPM" />

In [[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones|''Star Wars'': Episode II ''Attack of the Clones'']], the piece is played when the [[Padawan|Jedi Padawan]] Anakin Skywalker travels across the surface of the planet [[Tatooine]] to search for his mother, [[Shmi Skywalker Lars]], and rescue her from her captors, a tribe of a [[species]] called [[Tusken Raider]]s.<ref name="AOTC" />

In [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith|''Star Wars'': Episode III ''Revenge of the Sith'']], the theme is used as the [[Jedi Knight]] Anakin Skywalker crash-lands the ''[[Invisible Hand]]'' on [[Coruscant]],<ref name="ROTS" /> where an excerpt from the music used when Skywalker destroys the control ship in ''The Phantom Menace'' is utilized.<ref name="TPM" /> "Duel of the Fates" is played for the final time during the middle of the film's climax as Darth Vader [[Duel on Mustafar|duels]] against his former master Obi-Wan Kenobi on [[Mustafar]], as well as during [[Yoda]]'s [[Duel in the Galactic Senate|attempt to kill]] [[Darth Sidious]] in the [[Galactic Senate Chamber]] in an effort to bring his reign to an end and save the [[Galactic Republic]]. The piece concludes as Yoda falls from the [[Chancellor's podium]].<ref name="ROTS" />

In the [[2018]] [[Star Wars Anthology Series|Anthology]] film, ''[[Solo: A Star Wars Story]]'', the theme briefly recurs as Maul, revealed as the secret leader of [[Crimson Dawn]], instructs [[Qi'ra]] to begin dealing with him more personally.<ref name="Solo" /> While the theme appears briefly in the film, a longer statement is played in the deluxe edition of the [[Solo: A Star Wars Story (soundtrack)|soundtrack]].<ref name="SoloS">[[Solo: A Star Wars Story (soundtrack)|''Solo: A Star Wars Story'' (soundtrack)]]</ref>

===In other canon media===
Parts of "Duel of the Fates" appears in "[[Out of Darkness]]," the seventh episode of [[Star Wars Rebels Season One|Season One]] of ''[[Star Wars Rebels]]'', when the [[Spectres]] are attacked by [[fyrnock]]s.<ref name="Out of Darkness" /> The theme also appears in "[[Twilight of the Apprentice]]," the finale episode of the TV series' [[Star Wars Rebels Season Two|Season Two]], during the lightsaber duel between [[Ahsoka Tano]] and Darth Maul.<ref name="Twilight" /> The theme is also used extensively in the gameplay trailer for the [[2017]] [[EA Digital Illusions CE|DICE]] video game ''[[Star Wars Battlefront II]]'',<ref name="SWB2 trailer">{{EASWYouTube|_q51LZ2HpbE|Star Wars Battlefront II: Official Gameplay Trailer}}</ref> as well as in the game itself.<ref name="SWBII2017" />

===In Legends===
====Video games====
{{Listen2|Dotf_electronic.flac|"Duel of the Fates"|The electronic version of the theme from [[Star Wars: Episode I Racer|''Star Wars'': Episode I ''Racer'']]}}
The piece made its first [[Star Wars video games|video game]] appearance in the [[Star Wars: Episode I The Phantom Menace (video game)|''Star Wars'': Episode I ''The Phantom Menace'' video game]] during the game's last mission, titled "The Final Battle," detailing the duel between Maul, Kenobi, and Jinn.<ref name="TPMVG" /> A completely electronic version of the theme is used in the main menu for the 1999 [[Wikipedia:Game Boy Color|Game Boy Color]] game [[Star Wars: Episode I Racer|''Star Wars'': Episode I ''Racer'']], though the original recording is used in other versions of the game with the vocals cut.<ref name="SW1R" /> It also appears in the opening cinematic for ''[[Star Wars: Episode I Jedi Power Battles]]'' (2000), as well as that game's tenth mission, ''The Final Battle.''<ref name="JPB" /> The theme makes an appearance in the main menu of the game ''[[Star Wars: Episode I: Battle for Naboo]]'' (2000) and during the game's second bonus mission.<ref name="BFN" /> The composition is utilized in [[Star Wars: Starfighter (video game)|''Star Wars: Starfighter'']] ([[2001]]) during "The Final Assault" as rookie [[Pilot/Legends|pilot]] [[Rhys Dallows]] [[Starfighter combat/Legends|dogfights]] a [[Unidentified mercenary leader|mercenary leader]] in the space battle above Naboo.<ref name="SW:S" /> The motif is also used in the 2001 real-time strategy video game ''[[Star Wars: Galactic Battlegrounds]]''<ref name="GB" /> and its expansion pack [[Star Wars: Galactic Battlegrounds: Clone Campaigns|''Clone Campaigns'']], released in [[2002]].<ref name="GBCP" /> The composition is included in the level "Darth Maul" on the game ''[[Star Wars: Obi-Wan]]'' (2001).<ref name="SWOW" /> ''[[Star Wars: Jedi Starfighter]]'' (2002) uses different variations on the theme from various ''The Phantom Menace'' tracks in "Training Mission 5: Force Powers," "Mission 5: Poisoned Skies," "Mission 7:Hammer and Anvil," "Mission 8: Demolition Squad," "Mission 13: Attack of the Clones," and "Mission 15: The Jedi Master."<ref name="SWJS" /> The [[2003]] video game [[Star Wars: The Clone Wars (video game)|''Star Wars: The Clone Wars'']] uses the piece in "Mission 3: The Battle of Geonosis" as you assist in bringing down the second [[Core ship/Legends|core ship]], in "Mission 7: The Conquest of Raxus Prime" as you escort reinforcements, and in "Mission 16: Fate of the Republic" as you fight the [[Dark Reaper]].<ref name="SWTCW game" /> It can also be heard during the climactic [[Mission on Mustafar|battle]] between a [[Spacer/Legends|spacer]] and [[Vartonis|Lord Vartonis]] in [[Star Wars Galaxies: Trials of Obi-Wan|''Trials of Obi-Wan'']] ([[2005]]), the third expansion of the [[Wikipedia:Massively multiplayer online role-playing game|massively multiplayer online roleplaying game]] (MMORPG) ''[[Star Wars Galaxies: An Empire Divided]]''.<ref name="TOOW" /> Also in ''Galaxies'', players can choose to play "Duel of the Fates" from a [[Jukebox/Legends|jukebox]] playlist.<ref name="SWG" /> The theme is utilized in the sixth level of "Episode I"—titled "Darth Maul"—in the non-[[canon]] video game ''[[LEGO Star Wars: The Video Game]]'' (2005),<ref name="LSW" /> and in the sixth level of the same name in the non-canon ''[[LEGO Star Wars: The Complete Saga]]'' ([[2007]]).<ref name="LSWCS" /> It appears twice in the [[Star Wars Episode III: Revenge of the Sith (video game)|''Star Wars Episode III: Revenge of the Sith'' video game]] (2005), once in an introductory cinematic sequence featuring Obi-Wan Kenobi and Anakin Skywalker fighting in numerous story levels and again as the first piece that plays during the game's version of the Duel on Mustafar. It also appears in the GBA and DS versions for the same game, specifically during the Duel on Mustafar level, although its exact version varied: The former had a synthesized version of the theme being played as depicted from the film, and the latter had the vocals, although based on the version from ''Attack of the Clones''.<ref name="ROTSVG" />

It plays as [[Galen Marek]] fights [[PROXY]], who is disguised as [[Darth Maul/Legends|Darth Maul]] on [[Raxus Prime/Legends|Raxus Prime]] in the game ''[[Star Wars: The Force Unleashed]]'' ([[2008]]).<ref name="TFU" /> In the online game ''[[Ace Assault II]]'' ([[2011]]), the theme plays in levels four, ten, and sixteen, which pits Anakin Skywalker against [[Confederacy of Independent Systems/Legends|Separatist]] commanders [[Grievous/Legends|Grievous]] and [[Asajj Ventress/Legends|Asajj Ventress]] and Jedi Master [[Mace Windu/Legends|Mace Windu]] against the Sith Lord Darth Sidious.<ref name="AA2" /> A few sections from the composition were included in the [[Return (cinematic trailer)|''Return'']] trailer for the MMORPG ''[[Star Wars: The Old Republic]]'' (2011) during a lightsaber [[Battle of Korriban|duel]] between the Jedi [[Satele Shan]] and [[Kao Cen Darach]] and the Sith [[Darth Malgus]] and [[Darth Vindican|Vindican]].<ref name="SWTOR.COM" /> The MMORPG utilizes a cut from the track "Qui-Gon's Noble End" that contains the theme whenever a gamer playing as a [[Sith Warrior]] takes off in his or her [[Starship/Legends|starship]]. A brief motif is also used when the [[Darth Nox|Sith Inquisitor character]] kills [[Darth Zash]] and takes control of her [[Sith apprentice/Legends|Sith apprentices]].<ref name="TOR" /> The game ''[[Kinect Star Wars]]'' (2012) contains a game mode called "Duel of Fates." In the mode, players combat Sith or other sword-wielding opponents with a lightsaber. The game also features a dance move in the song "Hologram Girl" in the game's "Galactic Dance Off" mode titled "Duel of Fates." The move consists of arching one's left hand over their head while extending their right hand away from their body and facing their right palm out and perpendicular to their arm. The dancer then nods their head as it faces right on beats two and three. Next, the dancer hops to the right and switches arm positions so that the left hand is now extended and the right is arched. The head turns and faces left before nodding on beats two and three again.<ref name="KSW" /> An edited version is played on the boss battle levels of ''[[Angry Birds Star Wars II]]'' and on the main menu of "The Pork Side," the half of the game where the player plays for the [[Dark side of the Force/Legends|dark side]].<ref name="ABII" />

====Television====
The theme is played in ''[[Star Wars: Clone Wars]]'' Chapters [[Clone Wars Chapter 18|18]]<ref name="CW18" /> and [[Clone Wars Chapter 19|19]] ([[2004]]) during the [[Duel on Yavin 4|battle]] between Anakin Skywalker and [[Asajj Ventress/Legends|Asajj Ventress]] and concludes with Ventress's defeat.<ref name="CW19" /> In the non-[[canon]] special ''[[LEGO Star Wars: The Empire Strikes Out]]'', it is featured when Darth Maul first appears.<ref name="LEGOspecial" />

====Audio books====
The motif is also used as background music in the audio books for [[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones (novelization)|''Attack of the Clones'']]<ref name="AOTCAB">[[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones (novelization)|''Star Wars Episode II: Attack of the Clones'' audio book]]</ref> and [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith (novelization)|''Revenge of the Sith'']],<ref name="ROTSAB">[[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith (novelization)|''Star Wars Episode III: Revenge of the Sith'' audio book]]</ref> published in 2002<ref name="AOTCAB" /> and 2005 respectively. In the ''Revenge of the Sith'' audio book, the piece is played during the duel between Yoda and Palpatine in the Senate Retunda.<ref name="ROTSAB" /> Duel of the Fates is played while [[Mid Captain]] [[Ufsa'mak'ro]] orders the [[Kilji picket cruiser]] [[Hammer (Kilji picket cruiser)|''Hammer'']] to move aside of the [[Nikardun blockade frigate]] [[Aelos (frigate)|''Aelos'']] in [[Thrawn Ascendancy: Lesser Evil (audiobook)|''Thrawn Ascendancy: Lesser Evil''{{'s}} audiobook]].<ref name="LE">[[Thrawn Ascendancy: Lesser Evil (audiobook)|''Thrawn Ascendancy: Lesser Evil'' audiobook]]</ref>

====In-universe reference====
According to the roleplaying game sourcebook ''[[Coruscant and the Core Worlds]]'' (2003) written by [[Craig Robert Carey]], [[Chris Doyle]], [[Jason Fry]], [[Paul Sudlow]], [[John Terra]], and [[Daniel Wallace]], curators of the [[Coruscant/Legends|Coruscant]] [[Ice Crypts]] claim to have heard faint hums of "korah matah" emanating from the halls of the underground tombs.<ref name="CATCW">''[[Coruscant and the Core Worlds]]''</ref> Some believe this indicates that the tune may be of [[Zhell/Legends|Zhell]] origin [[in-universe]], as this is a phonetic excerpt of lyrics from "Duel of the Fates"<ref name="XF">{{WebCite|url=http://forums.spacebattles.com/threads/life-on-coruscant.215736|text=Life on Coruscant|author=Shighaara|date=2012-02-09|work=XenForo|archiveurl=https://archive.vn/G6gYD}}</ref> and it was believed that remains in the Ice Crypts were those of the chiefs of the thirteen nations of the Zhell.<ref name="CATCW" />

In the non-canon ''[[LEGO Star Wars: The Yoda Chronicles]]'' feature ''[[Menace of the Sith]]'', it is used in-universe as a theme played while the Sith [[Cloning/Legends|clone]] [[Jek-14/Legends|Jek-14]] demonstrates his skills, leading Maul to complain that Jek stole his theme song.<ref>''[[Menace of the Sith]]''</ref>

===In other media and merchandise===
[[File:PT_music_book.jpg|thumb|left|''Star Wars Episodes I, II & III Instrumental Solos'']]
The theme's sheet music appears in the sheet music books ''[[Music from Star Wars Episode I: The Phantom Menace]]'' (1999)<ref name="TPMMB" /> and ''[[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones: Selections from the Original Motion Picture Soundtrack]]'' (2002)<ref name="AOTCMB">[[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones: Selections from the Original Motion Picture Soundtrack]]</ref> from [[Bantha Music]]. The books are available for the following instruments: trumpet, piano, easy piano, clarinet, flute, [[Wikipedia:Tenor saxophone|tenor sax]], and [[Wikipedia:Alto saxophone|alto sax]],<ref name="TPMMB" /> and the ''Attack of the Clones'' book is also available for trombone.<ref name="AOTCMB" /> The piece also appears in the music books ''[[Star Wars Episodes I, II & III Instrumental Solos]]''<ref name="ISMB">''[[Star Wars Episodes I, II & III Instrumental Solos]]''</ref> and ''[[Star Wars: A Musical Journey: Episodes I-VI]]'' published by [[Alfred Publishing Co.]] in 2006 and 2007 respectively.<ref name="AMJMB">''[[Star Wars: A Musical Journey: Episodes I-VI]]''</ref> These two are available for trumpet, piano, easy piano, clarinet, flute, French horn, tenor sax, alto sax, trombone, violin, [[Wikipedia:Cello|cello]], and [[Wikipedia:Viola|viola]].<ref name="ISMB" /><ref name="AMJMB" /> The book ''[[Selections from Star Wars]]'', also from Alfred Publishing Co. and released [[July 1]], 1999, features sheet music for the theme. The book is available for both [[Wikipedia:Treble clef|treble clef]] and [[Wikipedia:Bass clef|bass clef]]. The treble clef version was the original, but the bass clef version, a piano accompaniment version, and versions in the keys of [[Wikipedia:C major|C]] and [[Wikipedia:B-flat major|B-flat]] were all released on [[September 1]], 1999. A version in the key of E-flat was released on [[January 1]], 2000. Each version contains arrangements for solos, duets, and trios. Arrangements in the treble clef version were done by Robert Shultz. All other versions have arrangements by Tony Esposito.<ref name="SFSWMB">''[[Selections from Star Wars]]''</ref> All the books from Alfred Publishing Co. also come with CDs with tracks playing the parts for each song.<ref name="ISMB" /><ref name="AMJMB" /><ref name="SFSWMB" />

Part of the piece is used in the first theatrical trailer for the 2008 animated film [[Star Wars: The Clone Wars (film)|''Star Wars: The Clone Wars'']].<ref name="TCWT1">[[Star Wars: The Clone Wars (film)|''Star Wars: The Clone Wars'']] first theatrical trailer</ref> A music video for the theme consists of clips from ''The Phantom Menace'' alongside video of the recording sessions for "Duel of the Fates" and was used as a trailer for the film.<ref name="Music video">{{SWArchive|url=episode-i/release/trailer/dotf.html|text=''Duel of the Fates'' Music Video}}</ref> This music video can also be played as a feature on the video game for ''The Phantom Menace''.<ref name="TPMVG" /> An [[Duel of the Fates (article)|article]] by [[Andy Collins]] in the [[Star Wars Gamer 1|''Star Wars Gamer'' 1]] magazine<ref name="SWG1">{{GamerCite|1|Duel of the Fates (article)}}</ref> and an [[Duel of the Fates (YJCCG)|expansion set]] for the [[Young Jedi Collectible Card Game]] both share the name "Duel of the Fates."<ref name="YJCCG">{{YJCCG|set=Duel of the Fates|parent=2}}</ref> The composition was utilized in the special features DVD of the 2004 [[original trilogy]] DVD box set in a featurette titled "Episode III," which gave a sneak peak of the then-upcoming ''Revenge of the Sith'' video game.<ref name="Featurette">"Episode III" featurette on the special features DVD of the [[2004]] [[original trilogy]] box set</ref> The theme was used in the [http://www.starwars.com/video/star-wars-rebels-full-trailer first] [http://www.starwars.com/video/star-wars-rebels-short-trailer two] official trailers for the animated series ''[[Star Wars Rebels]]'', released on May 4, [[2014]],<ref name="Rebels Full Trailer">{{SW|url=video/star-wars-rebels-full-trailer|text=Star Wars Rebels: Full Trailer}}</ref><ref name="Rebels Short Trailer">{{SW|url=video/star-wars-rebels-short-trailer|text=Star Wars Rebels: Short Trailer}}</ref> as well as an [http://www.starwars.com/video/star-wars-rebels-extended-trailer extended trailer] released on [[July 21]], 2014.<ref name="Rebels Extended Trailer">{{SW|url=video/star-wars-rebels-extended-trailer|text=''Star Wars Rebels'': Extended Trailer}}</ref>

===In Disney Parks===
"Duel of the Fates" was previously used in the ''[[Jedi Training Academy]]'' show at the Tomorrowland Terrace restaurant in [[Disneyland]] in [[Wikipedia:Anaheim, California|Anaheim, California]] when Darth Maul appeared from underneath the stage.<ref name="JTA DLR">{{YouTube|video=LmjgH0zyHP8|text=Star Wars Jedi Training Academy at Disneyland (Full Show)|channel=channel/UCv3SyD5sxl0ERK1oD3r4rkg|name=Positively Mommy|archiveurl=https://archive.vn/O0kfg}}</ref> The updated version of the show, called ''Jedi Training - Trials of the Temple'', uses the theme if Darth Maul appears instead of [[Ben Solo|Kylo Ren]] in both the Disneyland version<ref name="JTTOTT DLR">{{YouTube|video=DS2f8kTZdEY|text=Fight Lord Vader, Darth Maul, New Villain. Star Wars Jedi Training–Trials of the Temple, Disneyland|channel=channel/UCzsX6H1DlbS7BbnSzH7Y4cw|name=UndercoverTourist.com|archivedate=20200922082639}}</ref> and the version shown at [[Disney's Hollywood Studios]] at [[Wikipedia:Walt Disney World|Walt Disney World]].<ref name="JTTOTT WDW">{{YouTube|video=_Nsdx4d_ePQ|text=Darth Vader, Kylo Ren, Darth Maul, and The Seventh Sister - Trials of the Temple - Jedi show|channel=channel/UCwU9OOnWZMOgVFdipziJbMg|name=ReadySetLoL|archivedate=20191101160332}}</ref> The theme also begins playing during the first shots of [[Takodana]] during the nighttime show ''Star Wars: A Galactic Spectacular'', also at Disney's Hollywood Studios.<ref name="SWAGS">{{YouTube|video=fbz4Z3NOmWk|text=BEST VIEW - Star Wars: A Galactic Spectacular Fireworks | Disney's Hollywood Studios|channel=channel/UC7SjQGMropNWcVKwtwVQkbg|name=The DIS|archivedate=20200914071831}}</ref>

===In popular culture===
The composition has been featured on [[Wikipedia:The Simpsons|''The Simpsons'']] in the episode "[[Wikipedia:Please Homer, Don't Hammer 'Em...|Please Homer, Don't Hammer 'Em...]]" in which the characters [[Wikipedia:Bart Simpson|Bart Simpson]] and [[Wikipedia:Principal Skinner|Principal Skinner]] battle on top of a bus with sticks that have peanuts and shrimp attached to them.<ref name="Simpsons">{{YouTube|video=91nHhQVhWag|text=Bart vs Director Skinner|channel=channel/UCjNOVoTp9Fh4LfN0pQPw_fg|name=IKatakana|archivedate=20160101165833}}</ref> The theme also plays during ''[[Soulcalibur IV]]'' whenever a player chooses [[Galen Marek|Starkiller]] and fights within either of the game's two ''Star Wars''-themed stages, as well as during Starkiller's extended ending.<ref name="SC">''[[Soulcalibur IV]]''</ref> The piece is also included at the end of a [[Wikipedia:Verizon Wireless|Verizon Wireless]] commercial that features [[R2-D2]] as a promotion for the release of ''The Phantom Menace'' in 3-D.<ref name="Verizon">{{JCFCite|url=posts/32052226|board=Star Wars Saga In-Depth|thread=The Official Saga 3D Thread!!!|user=Luukeskywalker|date=2012-01-22|time=1:19 PM|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200802142317/https://boards.theforce.net/threads/the-official-saga-3d-thread.31282538/page-40|quote=Just watching the Ravens/Patriots game, and they just aired a Verizon Wireless commercial with a salesman and a customer in a Verizon store and R2D2 is right there and they are talking abour how R2 is from Naboo, etc. Then he demos the phone and it shows Maul vs. Obi-Wan with Duel of the Fates playing. Then after Verizon title screen, finasl screen shows Episode I in 3D only ine theaters.}}</ref> [[Wikipedia:The Piano Guys|The Piano Guys]] did a [[Wikipedia:List of musical medleys|medley]] of ''Star Wars'' themes called "Cellowars," in which "Duel of the Fates" was the most utilized motif.<ref name="Cellowars">{{YouTube|video=BgAlQuqzl8o|text=Cello Wars (Star Wars Parody) Lightsaber Duel - The Piano Guys|channel=channel/UCmKurapML4BF9Bjtj4RbvXw|name=ThePianoGuys|archivedate=20200912053252}}</ref>

==Reception==
The single lasted 11 days in the eighth position on [[Wikipedia:Total Request Live|Total Request Live's]] video debut countdown starting [[May 5]], 1999.<ref name="TRL">{{WebCite|url=http://atrl.net/trlarchive/?s=debuts|text=The TRL Archive: Debuts|work=ATRL.com|archivedate=20070928011304}}</ref>
